input.background: Refer to the completely randomized design of Example 9.4, in which we concluded that at least two of the four brands of golf balls are associated with different mean distances traveled when struck with a driver.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Table 6 & Pairwise Comparisons for Example 9.6 \\
\hline Brand Comparison & Confidence Interval \\
\hline $\left(\mu_{\mathrm{A}}-\mu_{\mathrm{B}}\ight)$ & (-15.82, -4.74) \\
\hline $\left(\mu_{\mathrm{A}}-\mu_{\mathrm{C}}\ight)$ & (-24.71, -13.63) \\
\hline ( $\mu_{\mathrm{A}}-\mu_{\mathrm{D}}$ ) & (-4.08, 7.00) \\
\hline ( $\mu_{\mathrm{B}}-\mu_{\mathrm{C}}$ ) & (-14.43, -3.35) \\
\hline ( $\mu_{\mathrm{B}}-\mu_{\mathrm{D}}$ ) & (6.20, 17.28) \\
\hline ( $\mu_{\mathrm{C}}-\mu_{\mathrm{D}}$ ) & (15.09, 26.17) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: Estimate the mean distance traveled for balls manufactured by the brand with the highest rank.

output.answer:
Brand C is ranked highest; thus, we want a confidence interval for μC. Because the samples were selected independently in a completely randomized design, a confidence interval for an individual treatment mean is obtained with the one-sample t confidence interval, using the mean square for error, MSE, as the measure of sampling variability for the experiment. A 95% confidence interval on the mean distance traveled by Brand C (apparently the 'longest ball' of those tested), is

$$
\bar{x}_{\mathrm{C}} \pm t_{.025} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{MSE}}{n}}
$$

where n = 10, t.025 ≈ 2 (based on 36 degrees of freedom), and MSE = 21.175 (obtained from the Excel printout, Figure 9.12). Substituting, we obtain

$$
270.0 \pm(2) \sqrt{\frac{21.175}{10}}=270.0 \pm 2.9 \text{ or }(267.1,272.9)
$$

Thus, we are 95% confident that the true mean distance traveled for Brand C is between 267.1 and 272.9 yards, when hit with a driver by Iron Byron.
